title: Variables, input, char and float
_comment: Reconstructed slide-for-slide from the retired LCS deck
Note the shape every content slide has there: a short heading, two or
three lines of in-class phrasing, and ONE coding window. That is what
'demo' slides are - they embed a single starting point from CMeCodeDir
directly. They deliberately do NOT frame the surrounding book page: that
prose is the pre-class reading, and re-projecting it in class is not the
'ref' slides with only=.activity are used where the class works a real
book activity, so the full problem statement comes from the book itself
and stays in sync with what students see at home.
Per-term ephemera from the original deck (that day's announcements, the
'who in this class...' slide, office-hour changes) is deliberately absent -
that is session-level, edited minutes before class without a rebuild.

slides.title: Where we left off
items: printf displays a message on the screen
\n starts a new line; quotes are escaped as \"
Comments: /* ... */ across lines, // to end of line

slides.title: What we learned
items: Variable names contain only letters, numbers and _
They must start with a letter or _
Variables must be initialized BEFORE they are used

slides.title: User input
body: It is possible to have the user enter values and store them in variables. The command for this is scanf().
Let's ask the user for their age and then print it out:

slides.title: Reading more than one value
slides.body: You can read multiple entries with one scanf statement if you wish — or simply use multiple scanf statements:

slides.title: Back to the multiplication table
slides.body: Now we'll have the user input which multiplication table to print.

slides.title: The char datatype
body: So far we have only stored integer numbers in variables. You can also store letters or symbols — characters.
The datatype is char and the format specifier is %c:

slides.title: User input — characters
slides.body: You can use scanf() to read a character from the user input:

slides.title: Decimal numbers
body: C has several data types for decimal numbers: float, double and long double, with increasing precision. We'll mostly use float, whose format specifier is %f.
Use %.1f, %.2f, … to set the number of decimal places — the default is 6.
